_purpose: Ground-level detail for Caelum City — what it actually looks, sounds, and smells like at street level. The writer's room reference. This is not lore summary; this is texture. Every scene set in this city should feel like the same city.
_tone_note: Pre-electricity but not primitive. NOT swords-and-castles. People have jobs, rent, bureaucracy, bad neighbors, credit problems, city politics. The fantasy elements are infrastructure — they're old, familiar, and mostly taken for granted. The world should feel as mundane and alive as contemporary fiction, just without electricity.

the_outer_districts.overall_feel: Think of a city neighborhood where the buildings are old, the rents are lower than they should be, the infrastructure is held together with familiarity and stubbornness, and the people who live here have decided not to leave. Not because it's good — because it's theirs. The outer districts are where you end up when the inner city costs too much, and where you stay when you realize the people here are better than the people who could afford not to be here.

the_streets.layout: Founding Age road grid underneath — perfectly level, water runs off in straight lines, never floods. The buildings on top of them are not Founding Age. They're generations of construction over construction: stone-and-timber three- and four-story buildings, narrow alleys between them, buildings that lean slightly toward each other across streets so narrow that the upper floors almost touch. Laundry lines. Awnings. Market stalls that fold out at dawn and back at dusk.
the_streets.the_paving: Ancient Founding Age stone beneath the surface. Where the newer paving cracked and wasn't repaired — which is most of the outer districts — you can see the older stone beneath. Smooth. Dark grey, almost black. It doesn't erode. People chip at it and the tools break. Some streets are repaired with ordinary stone on top of the ancient stuff, uneven, full of puddles after rain.
the_streets.light: Resonance lamps on the main streets — glass tubes the length of a forearm, filled with luminal residue, mounted in iron brackets on the buildings. They glow steadily: pale amber-white, not flickering. They last four to six months before the residue depletes. Replacing them is Guild-licensed work that costs more than most outer district residents earn in a week. When a lamp fades and goes dark, the city is supposed to replace it within three weeks. In the outer districts it takes three months. Consequence: the main streets have stretches of working lamps interrupted by dark patches where the broken ones haven't been replaced.
the_streets.the_dark_patches: When a lamp dies and stays dead, the area underneath it becomes something else. People use the dark patches. Market stalls for things that don't need Guild inspection. Kids use them for games that adults pretend not to see. Couples who don't want to be noticed. Occasionally: someone who needs to exchange something they couldn't exchange in the light. The Guild knows the dark patches exist. They check them when they're looking for something specific. Otherwise they leave them alone.
the_streets.sound: Constant low hum from the city's resonance infrastructure — audible if you stop to listen, background noise if you live here. Market noise during daylight: voices, animals, the specific sound of a skilled metalworker using resonance-assisted smithing (a clean ringing, not a clang). At night: quieter, but the hum gets more noticeable when other sounds fade. People who've lived here for years stop hearing it. Newcomers find it unsettling for the first few weeks.

the_outer_districts.the_smell: Coal smoke from the heating stoves — not wood, which costs too much; the outer districts burn low-grade coal that produces a thick greyish smoke. Street food from the Dusk Market and the morning bread stalls. The cold-stone mineral smell of Founding Age buildings, which is distinct from ordinary stone — slightly metallic, slightly warm, like heated flint. Underneath everything else: a faint charge in the air that nobody has a word for. Not unpleasant. Like the air before a storm, but constant. The people who've lived here longest don't notice it. Visitors sometimes comment on it and can't describe it. (Origin: the Founding Age infrastructure active beneath their feet.)

Kairo's flat.address: Third floor, 14 Ashburn Lane — above a grain storage unit that hasn't stored grain in forty years and is now used for a variety of storage by a variety of people who pay by the week
Kairo's flat.description: A single room with a low ceiling, a narrow window that faces an alley, a fold-out sleeping platform, a table with two chairs, a small coal stove with a cracked flue that lets smoke in on windy nights. The walls are Founding Age stone — the building was repurposed from something much older, and the inner walls have never needed repair in anyone's memory. The stone is dark and smooth and faintly warm to the touch even in winter. Nobody in the building talks about this.
Kairo's flat.the_drawings: Every available surface has been drawn on at some point. Chalk marks on the walls, charcoal on paper tacked over older paper, ink on the window ledge where there's good light. Mostly landscapes — places Kairo has never been that he could not describe if asked. Street layouts of cities with wrong geometry. Structures that look like Founding Age remnants but are not any remnant that exists. He rolls up the old drawings and puts them under the sleeping platform. He has stopped looking at them.
Kairo's flat.the_detail_nobody_notices: The walls of his flat sit over the densest intersection of Founding Age infrastructure in the entire outer district. The stone conducts something. People who sleep in this building consistently report unusually vivid dreams. Kairo has never mentioned this to anyone because he assumed it was normal.

Ren's Clock and Repair.address: Ground floor corner, 2 Ashburn Lane — two minutes' walk from Kairo's building, which is not a coincidence
Ren's Clock and Repair.description: A narrow shopfront, deep rather than wide, crammed with mechanisms. Clocks of every kind on every shelf — broken ones waiting for repair, repaired ones waiting for collection, finished ones that were never collected and have been ticking for years. The smell: machine oil, brass polish, and underneath it that same faint mineral warmth from the building's foundations. Ren works at a wide bench under the window, tools laid out in precise order, usually with three things in progress simultaneously.
Ren's Clock and Repair.the_business: Officially: mechanical repair — clocks, measuring instruments, door mechanisms, anything with moving parts. Unofficially: anything that needs to work and doesn't. Ren has picked up enough basic resonance mechanics from watching Kairo that he can diagnose resonance tool problems even though he cannot use resonance himself. He charges accordingly. The Guild has inspected his shop twice. Both times they found nothing licensing-relevant. He was polite and unhelpful and they left.
Ren's Clock and Repair.the_clock_collection: One wall is clocks he doesn't repair and doesn't sell. He says they're not for sale. He says they're examples. He does not explain which examples of what. Most of them are stopped at different times. A few keep running. None of them tell the same time. He winds them every morning. This has been noted by exactly one person who knows what it means, and that person has been trapped inside the Fracture for 2847 years.

The Dusk Market.location: Three interconnected alleys behind the Ninth Ward public bathhouse — technically uncovered by any market licensing, technically covered by the bathhouse's general-purpose permit, a legal ambiguity that has existed for sixty years and that nobody in city administration has incentive to resolve
The Dusk Market.description: Opens roughly two hours before sunset, packs up roughly two hours after. By unspoken agreement, no one who operates here asks where things come from. Fruit, cooked food, tools, clothing, information, unlicensed resonance work (minor — calming a disturbed resonance environment, reading an object's history, fixing a resonance lamp without going through Guild channels). Half the outer district shops here. So do three Guild junior inspectors, off duty, who everyone recognizes and nobody acknowledges.
The Dusk Market.kairo_here: Kairo does occasional work at the Dusk Market for a woman named Hedda who runs a resonance-object appraisal stall. She needs someone who can actually use Echo Sight to read object histories, which she cannot. He gets paid in food and occasional cash. It is the only work he does that uses his ability openly. He is careful about which objects he reads — some carry impressions so strong they knock him sideways for hours.

The Warden's Post.location: Corner of Ashburn Lane and the Market Road — deliberately visible, deliberately unimpressive
The Warden's Post.description: A two-room Guild liaison office for the outer districts. Notice board outside. Interior: a front room with a counter, a register of licensed Resonants and reported incidents, and whoever is currently on duty. Back room: records, a resonance-incident kit, and a cot because the overnight shifts happen more than they should.
The Warden's Post.current_warden: Inspector Tav — late thirties, seven years in this post, which is six more than anyone expected. She knows every repeat name in the outer district by memory. She has closed more incidents informally than she has officially filed. She is not corrupt. She is practical. She knows the difference between people who need help and people who need to be stopped, and she makes those distinctions herself rather than waiting for a form to tell her.
The Warden's Post.kairo_and_tav: Tav has Kairo's incident report from two years ago — the one that got him flagged as 'unstable and potentially hazardous.' She has looked at that report more than once. She is not sure she agrees with the flagging. She has not said this to anyone and is not sure she will. If something changes — if Kairo's name comes up again — her report will matter.

The Founding Gate.location: City centre, where the main road from the outer districts meets the inner city ring
The Founding Gate.description: A gate that opens at dawn and closes at dusk without being touched by anyone. It has done this every day for as long as anyone has records. The mechanism is Founding Age — smooth stone pillars, no visible hinge, no visible lock. City engineers have examined it four times in recorded history. None of them published conclusions. The gate is wide enough for a cart, tall enough that a person on horseback passes through without ducking.
The Founding Gate.the_shrine: People leave things at the base of the pillars. Small offerings — food, coins, written requests, flowers. There is no official religion associated with the gate. There is no agreed name for it. People in the outer districts call it the Waking Gate. People in the inner city call it the Old Door. Scholars call it Founding Age Structure VII. Nobody calls it what it actually is: the primary resonance anchor for the rebuilt suppression layer. When it eventually opens in the wrong direction, at the wrong time, everyone in the outer district will feel it before they see it.

the_guild_presence.how_it_feels_on_the_street: Not soldiers. Not constant. Present the way that authority is present in a neighborhood where people know the rules and know which rules get enforced. Guild inspectors come through the outer districts roughly twice monthly on license checks — verifying that anyone using resonance commercially has their credentials in order. Unlicensed resonance work isn't illegal exactly; unlicensed resonance work *charged for* is a Guild matter. The distinction is practically invisible and the Guild interprets it as they choose.
the_guild_presence.resonance_incidents: When something goes wrong with resonance — an untrained person causing an event, a residue buildup in a building, someone flagged as unstable having an episode — the Guild responds. Response time in the outer districts: two to four hours. In the inner city: twenty minutes. People in the outer districts have learned to handle the first two hours themselves.
the_guild_presence.the_flag_system: Anyone reported for 'uncontrolled resonance expression' gets a file. Three incidents means mandatory Guild evaluation. Evaluation outcomes: licensed training (rare, costs money), supervised restriction (you can use resonance but only registered methods), or classification as hazardous (very rare; means you cannot work with resonance at all and are subject to quarterly check-ins). Kairo has one incident on file. He has been careful to keep it at one.

kairo_daily_life.how_he_earns: Loading and carrying for market stalls in the mornings — cash in hand, no questions. Occasional runs for a messaging service that operates outside Guild channels (delivering things people don't want officially delivered). The Hedda work at the Dusk Market two or three evenings a week. Rarely: someone comes to Ren's shop looking for Kairo specifically, which means they need something resonance-related that they cannot get through official channels and cannot afford to report. He charges what they can pay. He mostly doesn't say no.
kairo_daily_life.the_work_he_won't_do: Anything for the people who collect debts in the outer districts. He did one job for them three years ago. He has not spoken about what he saw on that job. He has not done another job for them.
kairo_daily_life.the_visions: They come without warning and without scheduling. Standing in a market, carrying a crate, halfway through a conversation — the edges of the world blur slightly and something else overlaps. Not violent. Not painful. More like double vision: this place and another place occupying the same space. He cannot always tell if what he's seeing is real. He stopped telling people about it after the incident report.
kairo_daily_life.the_sketching: He has been doing it since he was twelve. He doesn't plan what he draws. He notices what appears on the page after. He has tried to stop — twice, once for eight months. Both times the visions got worse. He draws now without questioning it. He does not look at old drawings.

the_inner_city_contrast.feel: Cleaner. Quieter resonance hum — better maintained infrastructure. Resonance lamps that work and get replaced promptly. Wider streets. Buildings that are newer or maintained to look it. The Guild's main offices are here. So are the licensed professions, the merchants who trade with other cities, the people whose names appear in city records.
the_inner_city_contrast.the_divide: Not a wall. Not a checkpoint. A visible transition: the pavement changes quality, the buildings get taller and less crowded, the people's clothes are different. Guild inspectors in the inner city are more visible and move differently — purposeful rather than patrol. People here know which behavior draws attention and avoid it, because drawing attention here has faster consequences.
the_inner_city_contrast.what_kairo_thinks_of_it: He goes when he has to. He leaves when he can.
